label: Philosophical Zombie
definition: A hypothetical being physically and behaviorally identical to a conscious person but with no inner experience whatsoever; used by David Chalmers and others to argue that consciousness is not entailed by the physical facts. Its work is the conceivability argument: if a zombie is even conceivable, then consciousness could be absent while every physical fact stays the same, so consciousness is not reducible to the physical.

body.text: A philosophical zombie, or p-zombie, is a thought-experiment creature that is an exact physical and functional duplicate of a conscious human being but that has no conscious experience at all. It behaves identically, reports seeing colors and feeling pain, recoils from harm, discusses its inner life, and is molecule-for-molecule the same as its conscious twin; yet inside there is nothing, no qualia, no felt quality, the lights are off. The notion in its modern, carefully framed form is most associated with David Chalmers, especially "The Conscious Mind" (1996), though the broader idea of a being without inner experience has older antecedents. The p-zombie must be distinguished from the shambling movie monster; it is, by stipulation, behaviorally and physically perfect, differing from a conscious person in one respect only, the absence of experience.

The philosophical work the p-zombie does is to drive the conceivability argument against physicalism, the keeper. The argument runs roughly: if a philosophical zombie is conceivable, then it is metaphysically possible; if it is metaphysically possible, then a world physically identical to ours but lacking consciousness is possible; but if consciousness could be absent while every physical fact stays the same, then consciousness is not fixed by, and so not reducible to, the physical facts; therefore physicalism is false. The conclusion is that there is an explanatory gap between the physical and the experiential, which is precisely the hard-problem-of-consciousness. The zombie scenario also bears against functionalism and the computational-theory-of-mind specifically, since the zombie shares the conscious person's entire functional and computational organization yet, by hypothesis, lacks experience, which would show that functional or computational organization does not suffice for consciousness.

The argument is vigorously contested, and the disputes are instructive. A common line, taken by Daniel Dennett among others, denies that a philosophical zombie is genuinely conceivable: when you truly imagine a being functionally identical to a conscious person, including all its self-monitoring and its judgments that it is conscious, you have thereby imagined a conscious being, and the supposed extra fact you are subtracting is incoherent. Dennett presses the related point that the zombie's claim to be conscious would have exactly the same causes as ours, which should make us suspicious of our own conviction that there is a further fact. A second line, the type-B physicalist response, grants that zombies are conceivable but denies that conceivability entails metaphysical possibility, citing cases where what seems conceivable is nonetheless impossible. Defenders of the argument reply that consciousness is a special case where conceivability is an unusually good guide to possibility, precisely because there is no gap between how experience seems and how it is.

The philosophical zombie matters for AI as the limiting case that separates behavior and function from experience, and it generalizes a worry that machine minds make concrete. If a perfect functional duplicate of a human could lack all inner experience, then behavior, however sophisticated, is no proof of consciousness, and the question of whether any AI system is conscious cannot be answered by what it does or says. A system that passed every test, conversed about its feelings, and reported rich experience could still be, in effect, a philosophical zombie, with no one home. This is sobering for both the science and the ethics of advanced AI: it implies that no purely third-person criterion, and no degree of progress toward artificial-general-intelligence, could settle whether a machine has genuine experience or merely the outward signs of it. Whether the zombie is a profound revelation about the limits of physical explanation or a seductive but confused fiction remains one of the defining fault lines in the philosophy of mind.
